quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 7, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Advanced Ion and Atom Counting from Mass
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Converting Mass to Number of Ions/Atoms, Molar Mass Calculations, Avogadro's Number Applications, Counting Atoms in Complex Compounds

questions.question: 10.0 g of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) is used in a titration. How many hydroxide ions does this represent?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of NaOH

Molar mass NaOH = 40.00 g/mol
Moles NaOH = 10.0 / 40.00 = 0.250 mol

Step 2: Find moles of OH⁻ ions

Each NaOH gives 1 OH⁻ ion
Moles OH⁻ = 0.250 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of OH⁻ ions

Number = 0.250 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.51 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: A chemist burns 3.60 g of propane (C₃H₈). Calculate the number of carbon atoms involved.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of C₃H₈

Molar mass C₃H₈ = 44.09 g/mol
Moles = 3.60 / 44.09 = 0.0817 mol

Step 2: Find moles of C atoms

Each C₃H₈ has 3 C atoms
Moles C = 0.0817 × 3 = 0.245 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of C atoms

Number = 0.245 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.48 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 2.50 g of calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂) is dissolved. Find the number of oxygen atoms present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of Ca(OH)₂

Molar mass Ca(OH)₂ = 74.10 g/mol
Moles = 2.50 / 74.10 = 0.0337 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O atoms

Each Ca(OH)₂ has 2 O atoms
Moles O = 0.0337 × 2 = 0.0674 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O atoms

Number = 0.0674 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $4.06 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 11.7 g of sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) is added to water. How many sodium ions are produced?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of Na₂CO₃

Molar mass Na₂CO₃ = 105.99 g/mol
Moles = 11.7 / 105.99 = 0.1104 mol

Step 2: Find moles of Na⁺ ions

Each Na₂CO₃ gives 2 Na⁺ ions
Moles Na⁺ = 0.1104 × 2 = 0.2208 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of Na⁺ ions

Number = 0.2208 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.33 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: A student weighs 6.60 g of urea (CO(NH₂)₂). Determine the number of nitrogen atoms present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of urea

Molar mass CO(NH₂)₂ = 60.06 g/mol
Moles = 6.60 / 60.06 = 0.110 mol

Step 2: Find moles of N atoms

Each urea has 2 N atoms
Moles N = 0.110 × 2 = 0.220 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of N atoms

Number = 0.220 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.32 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 5.40 g of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) decomposes. Calculate the number of oxygen atoms involved.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of H₂O₂

Molar mass H₂O₂ = 34.02 g/mol
Moles = 5.40 / 34.02 = 0.1587 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O atoms

Each H₂O₂ has 2 O atoms
Moles O = 0.1587 × 2 = 0.3174 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O atoms

Number = 0.3174 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.91 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 9.00 g of iron(III) oxide (Fe₂O₃) is reduced in a furnace. How many iron atoms are present?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of Fe₂O₃

Molar mass Fe₂O₃ = 159.70 g/mol
Moles = 9.00 / 159.70 = 0.05635 mol

Step 2: Find moles of Fe atoms

Each Fe₂O₃ has 2 Fe atoms
Moles Fe = 0.05635 × 2 = 0.1127 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of Fe atoms

Number = 0.1127 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $6.79 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 1.50 g of lithium fluoride (LiF) is used in a reaction. Determine the number of fluoride ions.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of LiF

Molar mass LiF = 25.94 g/mol
Moles = 1.50 / 25.94 = 0.0578 mol

Step 2: Find moles of F⁻ ions

Each LiF gives 1 F⁻ ion
Moles F⁻ = 0.0578 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of F⁻ ions

Number = 0.0578 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $3.48 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: A student dissolves 7.92 g of copper(II) sulfate (CuSO₄). How many copper ions are present?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of CuSO₄

Molar mass CuSO₄ = 159.61 g/mol
Moles = 7.92 / 159.61 = 0.0496 mol

Step 2: Find moles of Cu²⁺ ions

Each CuSO₄ gives 1 Cu²⁺ ion
Moles Cu²⁺ = 0.0496 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of Cu²⁺ ions

Number = 0.0496 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $2.99 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 4.40 g of carbon monoxide (CO) is collected in a gas jar. Calculate the number of oxygen atoms present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of CO

Molar mass CO = 28.01 g/mol
Moles = 4.40 / 28.01 = 0.1571 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O atoms

Each CO has 1 O atom
Moles O = 0.1571 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O atoms

Number = 0.1571 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $9.46 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 13.5 g of zinc chloride (ZnCl₂) is prepared. How many chloride ions are present?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of ZnCl₂

Molar mass ZnCl₂ = 136.28 g/mol
Moles = 13.5 / 136.28 = 0.0991 mol

Step 2: Find moles of Cl⁻ ions

Each ZnCl₂ gives 2 Cl⁻ ions
Moles Cl⁻ = 0.0991 × 2 = 0.1982 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of Cl⁻ ions

Number = 0.1982 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.19 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: A reaction uses 2.20 g of nitrogen gas (N₂). Determine the number of nitrogen atoms involved.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of N₂

Molar mass N₂ = 28.02 g/mol
Moles = 2.20 / 28.02 = 0.0785 mol

Step 2: Find moles of N atoms

Each N₂ has 2 N atoms
Moles N = 0.0785 × 2 = 0.157 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of N atoms

Number = 0.157 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $9.45 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 16.0 g of oxygen gas (O₂) is supplied to a reaction vessel. How many oxygen molecules are present?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of O₂

Molar mass O₂ = 32.00 g/mol
Moles = 16.0 / 32.00 = 0.500 mol

Step 2: Calculate number of O₂ molecules

Number = 0.500 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $3.01 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 3.18 g of sodium phosphate (Na₃PO₄) is dissolved in water. Calculate the number of phosphate ions present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of Na₃PO₄

Molar mass Na₃PO₄ = 163.94 g/mol
Moles = 3.18 / 163.94 = 0.0194 mol

Step 2: Find moles of PO₄³⁻ ions

Each Na₃PO₄ gives 1 PO₄³⁻ ion
Moles PO₄³⁻ = 0.0194 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of PO₄³⁻ ions

Number = 0.0194 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.17 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: A person adds 5.70 g of sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) to a solution. What is the number of hydrogen atoms added?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of sucrose

Molar mass C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ = 342.30 g/mol
Moles = 5.70 / 342.30 = 0.01665 mol

Step 2: Find moles of H atoms

Each sucrose has 22 H atoms
Moles H = 0.01665 × 22 = 0.3663 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of H atoms

Number = 0.3663 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $2.21 \times 10^{23}$

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered advanced ion and atom counting! 🧪🔬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the steps: mass → moles → particles, paying attention to atom ratios.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on calculating molar mass correctly and identifying atom ratios in formulas.
scoreMessages.0: Please review molar mass calculations, mole concept, and Avogadro's number applications.
